title: Spectral invariants for contactomorphisms of prequantization bundles and applications
speaker: Frol Zapolsky
abstract: I'll outline the construction and computation of a Floer homology theory for contact manifolds which are prequantization spaces over monotone symplectic manifolds, and of the spectral invariants resulting therefrom, and present some applications.

proof_stages.label: Prequantization contact setting and contact Hamiltonians
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a prequantization circle bundle over a closed positively monotone symplectic manifold and identifies the relevant universal cover of the contactomorphism group via contact Hamiltonians and their flows.

proof_stages.label: Floer package for the contact manifold
proof_stages.summary: A unital associative Frobenius Floer homology algebra HF(V) is introduced and later realized as Lagrangian Floer homology of a canonical Lagrangian in the product of the symplectization of V with the base, with contact Hamiltonians entering as Hamiltonian perturbations.

proof_stages.label: Gysin-type computation and vanishing criterion
proof_stages.summary: The Floer homology is computed through a long exact sequence relating quantum homology of the base to HF(V), yielding the criterion that HF(V) vanishes exactly when the Poincare dual of the Euler class is quantum-invertible.

proof_stages.label: Translated points, discriminant points, and translated spectrum
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines translated points and discriminant points for a contactomorphism and packages their Reeb-time data into a translated spectrum, periodic with the Reeb fiber length.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-invariant theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts spectral invariants l(a,phi) for Floer classes and contactomorphisms, satisfying finiteness, monotonicity, triangle inequality, duality, comparison with Hamiltonian spectral invariants under lifts, Reeb-flow normalization, spectrality, and a continuity estimate.

proof_stages.label: Reeb-loop and orderability consequences
proof_stages.summary: Assuming HF(V) is nonzero, the Reeb loop gives an injected copy of Z in the fundamental group of the contactomorphism group, and monotonicity of the fundamental-class spectral invariant rules out positive contractible loops, giving orderability.

proof_stages.label: Existence of translated points for arbitrary contact forms
proof_stages.summary: For any contact form defining the same contact structure and any contactomorphism isotopic to the identity, the proof detects a translated point by composing with negative Reeb flow, using spectrality to recognize discriminant points when l^+ hits multiples of the fiber length, and using continuity plus monotonic estimates to force such a hit.

examples.name: Hopf prequantization of complex projective space
examples.note: The Hopf fibration over CP^n has Euler class Poincare dual to the hyperplane class, which is invertible in quantum homology, illustrating the vanishing criterion and matching the standard sphere's non-orderability.
examples.engagement_reason: The Hopf prequantization is used to identify the Euler class with the hyperplane class, check it is invertible, and conclude the constructed Floer homology vanishes.

examples.name: Real projective prequantization space
examples.note: The prequantization with Euler class twice the hyperplane class has zero Euler class over Z_2, so the Floer homology is nonzero, illustrating the nonvanishing/orderability side of the criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: For the real projective prequantization, the speaker computes the Euler class as twice the generator, reduces it to zero over Z2, and applies the vanishing criterion to conclude nonvanishing Floer homology.

examples.name: Largest prequantization over CP^1 x CP^1
examples.note: The prequantization with Euler class (1,1) has noninvertible Poincare dual and total space diffeomorphic to S^2 x S^3, showing that nonvanishing is not simply explained by the Reeb fiber's fundamental-group behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: For the largest prequantization over CP^1 x CP^1, the speaker identifies the Poincare dual Euler class as (1,1), checks non-invertibility in quantum homology, and derives nonvanishing of the Floer homology.

examples.name: Sphere and projective-space cases of the translated-point conjecture
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker compares the theorem with known or open translated-point results for standard contact spheres and projective spaces, including the generating-function approach in the standard case.
examples.engagement_reason: The sphere and projective-space translated-point cases are discussed only as known or open cases and by reference to methods such as generating functions, without carrying out a computation or verification on those cases.
